## VI. Provision 4 — Exit Valuation Methodology (Article X)

### A. Summary of Positions

**Meridian's opening position.** A 6.5× trailing 12-month EBITDA multiple, with the JV's own auditor (Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP) serving as the sole valuation appraiser, and trigger events limited to change of control and material breach. The rationale is that using the existing auditor reduces cost and leverages institutional knowledge, and that 6.5× is a conservative, defensible multiple for a development-stage venture.

**Kastor's opening position.** An independent Big Four appraiser (not Frazier Whitmore) selected through a three-appraiser baseball-arbitration mechanism, with a market-determined EBITDA multiple, and trigger events including change of control, material breach, deadlock, and involuntary transfer. The rationale is that an entity serving as the JV's auditor has a structural conflict of interest in also serving as the buyout valuation appraiser, and that the integrity of the buyout mechanism depends on genuine independence.

**Areas of identified flexibility.** Meridian signaled willingness to move the multiple up to 7.0× (near the 7.1× median of the Frazier Whitmore comparable-transactions data) and to consider an independent appraiser mechanism, though it preferred a simpler selection process than three-appraiser baseball arbitration. Meridian also signaled willingness to add deadlock and involuntary transfer as trigger events. Kastor signaled flexibility on the specific multiple number (within the 6.5×–7.5× range) and willingness to consider a simplified appraiser-selection mechanism, but maintained that appraiser independence from Frazier Whitmore is non-negotiable.

### B. Proposed Compromise Clause Language

The following language is proposed to replace Sections 10.01, 10.03, and 10.04 of the JVA. Section 10.02 (Buyout Procedure) is retained as drafted in JVA v.4. The defined term "Fair Market Value" in Article I is amended as noted.

> **"Fair Market Value"** means the value determined in accordance with Section 10.03 by an Independent Appraiser selected in accordance with Section 10.03(b).

> **Section 10.01 — Triggering Events**
>
> A buyout or dissolution procedure under this Article X shall be initiated upon the occurrence of one of the following events (each, a "**Triggering Event**"):
>
> **(a)** A Change of Control of either Member;
>
> **(b)** A Material Breach by either Member that remains uncured after the applicable cure period set forth in Section 11.02;
>
> **(c)** A Deadlock that has not been resolved through the escalation and mediation procedures set forth in Section 4.04(a) and (b); or
>
> **(d)** An Involuntary Transfer of all or any portion of a Member's Membership Interest (by operation of law, bankruptcy, judicial order, or otherwise).
>
> For the avoidance of doubt, the following shall not constitute a Triggering Event: (i) a reorganization, recapitalization, or other internal restructuring of a Member's corporate structure that does not change the ultimate beneficial ownership or Control of such Member's Membership Interest, including a transfer of a Member's Membership Interest to a wholly owned Affiliate; or (ii) a merger, consolidation, or similar transaction involving a Member's parent company, provided that the surviving entity assumes all of the Member's obligations under this Agreement.

> **Section 10.03 — Valuation**
>
> **(a) Methodology.** The Fair Market Value of the Company shall be determined by applying a multiple of seven and zero-tenths times (7.0×) the Company's trailing twelve (12) month Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization ("**EBITDA**") as of the last day of the most recently completed Fiscal Quarter preceding the date of the Triggering Event. For purposes of this calculation, EBITDA shall be determined on a consolidated basis in accordance with GAAP, and shall exclude any extraordinary, non-recurring, or unusual items, as determined by the Independent Appraiser. The Independent Appraiser may use discounted cash flow analysis and comparable transaction analysis as supplementary cross-check methodologies, but the EBITDA-multiple method set forth in this Section 10.03(a) shall be the primary and determinative valuation methodology.
>
> **(b) Selection of Independent Appraiser.**
>
> (i) The Members shall endeavor to agree upon a single nationally recognized independent accounting or valuation firm (a "Big Four" firm or equivalent) to serve as the "**Independent Appraiser**" within thirty (30) calendar days following the occurrence of the applicable Triggering Event.
>
> (ii) If the Members are unable to agree on a single Independent Appraiser within such thirty (30)-day period, each Member shall, within fifteen (15) calendar days thereafter, select one nationally recognized independent accounting or valuation firm, and the two firms so selected shall, within fifteen (15) calendar days of their appointment, jointly select a third nationally recognized independent accounting or valuation firm to serve as the Independent Appraiser.
>
> (iii) If the two firms cannot agree on a third firm within the fifteen (15)-day period specified above, either Member may apply to the American Arbitration Association for the appointment of the Independent Appraiser.
>
> (iv) The Independent Appraiser shall not be the Company's then-current auditor (currently Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP) or any firm that has provided material services to either Member or any of its Affiliates during the preceding twenty-four (24) months.
>
> (v) The Independent Appraiser shall deliver its written determination of EBITDA and Fair Market Value to both Members within forty-five (45) days after being engaged to perform such determination.
>
> **(c) Baseball Arbitration.** If either Member disputes the Independent Appraiser's determination of Fair Market Value, the following procedure shall apply:
>
> (i) Each Member shall, within twenty (20) calendar days of receipt of the Independent Appraiser's report, submit to the Independent Appraiser a single sealed final valuation number (each, a "**Final Offer**").
>
> (ii) The Independent Appraiser shall select the Final Offer that is closest to its own determination of Fair Market Value, and the selected Final Offer shall be the Fair Market Value for all purposes under this Agreement.
>
> (iii) The Independent Appraiser's selection shall be final and binding upon the Members, absent fraud or manifest error.
>
> (iv) **Multiple Collar.** Any valuation submitted by the Independent Appraiser or as a Final Offer under this Section 10.03(c) must imply an EBITDA multiple within the range of six and five-tenths times (6.5×) to seven and five-tenths times (7.5×) (the "**Collar**"). Any valuation that implies a multiple outside the Collar shall be deemed non-compliant and shall be excluded from the baseball arbitration process.
>
> **(d) Member Interest Valuation.** The Fair Market Value of a Member's Membership Interest shall equal the Fair Market Value of the Company multiplied by such Member's then-current Percentage Interest. No minority discount or marketability discount shall be applied.
>
> **(e) Costs.** The costs and expenses of the Independent Appraiser shall be borne equally by the Members.

> **Section 10.04 — Payment Terms**
>
> The purchase price determined pursuant to Section 10.03 shall be payable by the Initiating Member to the Selling Member at the closing of the buyout as follows: (i) at least seventy percent (70%) of the purchase price shall be paid in cash by wire transfer of immediately available funds to an account designated by the Selling Member; and (ii) at the Initiating Member's election, up to thirty percent (30%) of the purchase price may be paid in the form of a promissory note issued by the Initiating Member (or its designee) in favor of the Selling Member, bearing interest at the prime rate as published by *The Wall Street Journal* on the date of closing, plus two percent (2.00%) per annum, payable in equal quarterly installments of principal and accrued interest over a period of twenty-four (24) months following the closing date, with the first installment due ninety (90) days after the closing date. Such promissory note shall be unsecured unless otherwise agreed by the parties and shall contain customary terms and conditions, including provisions for acceleration upon default.

### C. Negotiation Rationale

**Why this is a fair middle ground.** The compromise resolves the two sub-issues — the multiple and the appraiser — at points that respect each party's top priority on this provision while constraining the outcome within market-supported bounds.

*The multiple: 7.0× as the anchor, within a 6.5×–7.5× collar.* Meridian moves from its 6.5× opening to 7.0× — a 0.5× increase that brings the multiple to the market center. The Frazier Whitmore comparable-transactions analysis shows a range of 6.2× to 8.1× with a median of 7.1×; the three JV-formation comparables in that dataset traded at 7.1×, 7.4×, and 7.3×. At 7.0×, the multiple is within 0.1× of the median and is supported by Comparable Transaction #1 (6.8×) and Comparable Transaction #3 (7.1×). This is a meaningful, data-driven concession by Meridian that can be presented as conceding to the market center. The 6.5×–7.5× collar — tighter than Meridian's fallback 6.0×–8.0× collar — protects both parties against outlier results: Meridian is protected against a lowball below 6.5× (above Meridian's 6.0× walk-away floor), and Kastor retains upside to 7.5× if the Independent Appraiser's analysis supports it. Because either party could be the buyer or the seller at exit, the collar is symmetric in expectation.

*The appraiser: independent, not Frazier Whitmore.* This is Kastor's top priority on this provision and is conceded by Meridian. The compromise adopts Kastor's minimum acceptable position: an independent appraiser that is not Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP and has no pre-existing engagement with either Member or the Company in the preceding 24 months. The selection mechanism is a simplified version of Kastor's proposed three-appraiser process: the parties first endeavor to mutually agree on a single firm (the simplest path); if they cannot, each selects one firm and those two select a third; if they still cannot agree, the AAA appoints. This is less complex than full three-appraiser baseball arbitration but preserves the independence Kastor requires. Meridian's preference for a simpler mechanism is honored by making mutual agreement the first step. The independence concern is legitimate: an entity serving as the JV's auditor maintains an ongoing commercial relationship with the Company and, through the Meridian-controlled management structure, a reporting relationship to a Meridian-aligned management team; in a buyout scenario where the members' interests are directly adverse, this dual role creates an inherent conflict. The Frazier Whitmore comparable-transactions data remains a useful reference that any competent independent appraiser would consider, but the firm itself does not serve as the valuation appraiser.

*Baseball arbitration with collar.* If either party disputes the Independent Appraiser's determination, baseball arbitration applies: each party submits a single sealed Final Offer, and the appraiser selects the offer closest to its own determination. Baseball arbitration incentivizes both parties to submit reasonable figures (an extreme position risks being passed over), and the collar (6.5×–7.5×) confines the outcome to the market-supported range. This is a fair, market-based dispute-resolution mechanism that neither party can game.

*Methodology: EBITDA primary, DCF as cross-check only.* Meridian's walk-away requirement — that trailing 12-month EBITDA be the foundational metric and that DCF not be the primary or sole methodology — is preserved. DCF valuations are susceptible to manipulation of growth-rate, discount-rate, and terminal-value assumptions, which is particularly dangerous in a disputed exit. The Independent Appraiser may use DCF and comparable transaction analysis as supplementary cross-checks (giving Kastor some of the methodological flexibility it sought), but the EBITDA-multiple method is primary and determinative. The Frazier Whitmore DCF-vs.-multiple analysis shows that DCF and EBITDA-multiple valuations converge within a -7.1% to +9.7% range (mean absolute variance 6.5%), confirming that EBITDA-multiple valuation is a reliable primary methodology with DCF as a reasonable cross-check.

*No discounts.* The compromise adopts Kastor's position that no minority or marketability discounts be applied. In a negotiated JV exit where the buyout price is the Fair Market Value of the whole Company multiplied by the exiting member's Percentage Interest, applying minority or marketability discounts would undercompensate the selling member. Because either party could be the seller, the no-discount rule is symmetric in expectation and is the fairer approach for a jointly negotiated exit mechanism.

*Trigger events: four triggers with restructuring carve-outs.* The compromise adds deadlock and involuntary transfer as trigger events (Kastor's position), which Meridian signaled willingness to accept. The deadlock trigger is consistent with the tiered deadlock resolution in Provision 1 (a Reserved Matter deadlock that survives escalation and mediation becomes a buyout trigger). The involuntary-transfer trigger protects both parties against being forced to remain in a JV with an unknown counterparty (e.g., a bankruptcy trustee or creditor). Meridian's requested carve-outs for intra-group restructurings (transfers to wholly owned affiliates that do not change beneficial ownership, and parent-company mergers where the surviving entity assumes the member's obligations) are included, protecting both parties from triggering a buyout through ordinary corporate housekeeping.

*Cost-sharing.* The Independent Appraiser's costs are borne equally by the Members (Kastor's position), which is appropriate for a valuation that serves the buyout mechanism benefiting both parties.

### D. Cross-References

- **Section 4.04(c)(ii) (Deadlock):** The deadlock forced-buyout in Provision 1 constitutes a Triggering Event under Section 10.01(c), linking the governance and exit provisions coherently.
- **Section 5.01(d)(vi) (Forced-Sale Backstop):** The capital-call forced-sale backstop uses the Fair Market Value methodology in Section 10.03, ensuring consistent valuation across all exit triggers.
- **Section 9.06 (Involuntary Transfers):** The involuntary-transfer trigger in Section 10.01(d) operates alongside the buyout provisions of Section 9.06.
- **Section 8.02 (Auditor):** Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP remains the Company's independent auditor for annual financial-statement audits; the independence requirement in Section 10.03(b)(iv) applies only to the exit-valuation appraiser role, not to the audit engagement.
- **Article I (Definitions):** "Fair Market Value" is amended to reference the Independent Appraiser; "Triggering Event" is amended to include Deadlock and Involuntary Transfer; new defined terms "Independent Appraiser," "Final Offer," and "Collar" are added.

## VII. Provision 5 — IP License-Back and Co-Developed IP Rights (Article XII)

### A. Summary of Positions

**Meridian's opening position.** An exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to all co-developed IP granted to Meridian only, with no sublicensing rights for Kastor and no license of any kind to Kastor with respect to Meridian's 23 contributed IP items after exit. The rationale is that Meridian's contributed IP ($28.6M, including proprietary coating formulations embedded in JV products) is the JV's core competitive differentiator, and any structure that allows Kastor to sublicense that IP — or co-developed IP derived from it — to Meridian's North American competitors would destroy Meridian's competitive moat.

**Kastor's opening position.** A non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to all co-developed IP granted to both parties, with territorial sublicensing rights (Meridian in North America; Kastor in the rest of the world), and improvements to contributed IP treated as co-developed IP. The rationale is that Kastor's $43.8M contribution (including approximately $41.8M in cash that directly funds JV R&D) entitles Kastor to meaningful rights in the intellectual property created during the JV's life, and that without such rights Kastor would have funded the creation of valuable IP and then been unable to use it.

**Areas of identified flexibility.** Meridian signaled willingness to accept non-exclusive licenses to both parties for internal use, and limited flexibility on co-developed IP licensing outside North America and on the treatment of improvements, provided no structure creates a pathway for Meridian's formulations to reach North American competitors. Kastor signaled willingness to accept a prohibition on sublicensing to entities on a mutually agreed Restricted Competitors schedule (Schedule 12.05), provided the restriction is reciprocal, limited to specifically identified entities, and updated annually. Both sides flagged the "improvements gap" — the treatment of JV-funded improvements to contributed IP — as an issue requiring specific resolution.

**Common ground.** Both sides agree that contributed IP reverts to the contributing party upon exit or dissolution. This is not in dispute.

### B. Proposed Compromise Clause Language

The following language is proposed to replace Sections 12.03 and 12.04 of the JVA, and to add a new Section 12.06 addressing improvements to contributed IP. Sections 12.01 (Contributed IP), 12.02 (Co-Developed IP; Ownership), and 12.05 (Restricted Competitors) are retained with the conforming amendments to Schedule 12.05 noted in the rationale.

> **Section 12.03 — Reversion of Contributed IP; Transitional License**
>
> **(a) Reversion.** Upon the dissolution of the Company, termination of this Agreement, or an Exit Event, all Contributed IP shall automatically revert to the contributing Member, free and clear of any lien, encumbrance, security interest, or other restriction created by or through the Company. Such reversion shall be effective immediately upon the applicable event without the requirement of any further action, instrument, agreement, or consent by the Company, any Member, or any other Person. For the avoidance of doubt, upon such reversion, the Company's license to the Contributed IP under Section 12.01(c) shall terminate.
>
> **(b) Transitional License.** Notwithstanding Section 12.03(a), upon the dissolution of the Company, termination of this Agreement, or an Exit Event, each Member hereby grants to the other Member a non-exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable, worldwide license to use such granting Member's Contributed IP solely for the purpose of permitting the orderly wind-down of the Company's operations and the transition of each Member to standalone operations, for a period of twelve (12) months following the effective date of the applicable event (the "**Transitional Period**"). Upon expiration of the Transitional Period, such transitional license shall terminate automatically, and no ongoing license to the other Member's Contributed IP shall be granted or implied, except as expressly provided in Section 12.06 with respect to improvements to Contributed IP.
>
> **(c) No Ongoing Contributed-IP Cross-License.** For the avoidance of doubt, except for the Transitional License in Section 12.03(b) and the improvements license in Section 12.06, no license of any kind — exclusive, non-exclusive, or otherwise — is granted to either Member with respect to the other Member's Contributed IP following the expiration of the Transitional Period. Each Member's Contributed IP remains the exclusive property of the contributing Member following reversion.

> **Section 12.04 — Co-Developed IP License-Back**
>
> **(a) License to Both Members.** Upon the dissolution of the Company, termination of this Agreement, or an Exit Event, the Company (or its successor or assign) hereby grants to each Member a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable license to use, make, have made, sell, offer to sell, import, and otherwise exploit all Co-Developed IP (the "**License-Back**"). The License-Back is granted to both Members on equivalent terms.
>
> **(b) Territorial Scope.** Each Member's License-Back shall extend to such Member's respective territory:
>
> (i) **Meridian's Territory:** the Americas, meaning North America (the United States, Canada, and Mexico), Central America, South America, and the Caribbean.
>
> (ii) **Kastor's Territory:** all countries and territories outside the Americas (the "**Rest of World**").
>
> **(c) Sublicensing.** Each Member shall have the right to sublicense the Co-Developed IP within its respective territory to any third party, provided that such sublicense is consistent with the territorial limitations set forth in Section 12.04(b) and the restrictions set forth in Sections 12.04(d) and 12.04(e).
>
> **(d) Restricted Competitors Covenant.** Neither Member shall sublicense Co-Developed IP to any entity listed on Schedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors), or any controlled Affiliate of such an entity (meaning an entity in which a Restricted Competitor owns, directly or indirectly, fifty percent (50%) or more of the voting equity), regardless of the territory in which the sublicense would be exercised. This restriction applies reciprocally to both Members.
>
> **(e) Revenue-Based Negative Covenant.** In addition to the Restricted Competitors covenant in Section 12.04(d), neither Member shall sublicense Co-Developed IP to any entity that derives more than twenty-five percent (25%) of its consolidated annual revenue from the manufacture, sale, or distribution of specialty polymer coatings in North America. This restriction applies reciprocally to both Members and operates as a dynamic anti-circumvention mechanism to capture entities not listed on Schedule 12.05, including new market entrants, entities that expand into North American specialty coatings after the date of this Agreement, and entities that restructure to avoid the named-entity list.
>
> **(f) No Cross-Territorial Rights.** Neither Member shall exercise or sublicense any rights under the License-Back in the other Member's territory without the prior written consent of such other Member.
>
> **(g) No Exclusive License.** The License-Back granted under this Section 12.04 is non-exclusive. Neither Member shall receive an exclusive license to any Co-Developed IP in any territory.
>
> **(h) Audit Right.** Each Member shall have the right, upon reasonable prior written notice and at its own expense, to audit the other Member's sublicensing records no more than once per calendar year to verify compliance with the territorial limitations and the Restricted Competitors and revenue-based covenants set forth in this Section 12.04. The audited Member shall reasonably cooperate with such audit and shall make its sublicensing records available during normal business hours. If an audit reveals a material breach of the restrictions in this Section 12.04, the auditing Member's reasonable audit costs shall be reimbursed by the audited Member.
>
> **(i) Schedule 12.05 Maintenance.** Schedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors) shall identify specific entities by name and shall not extend to broad categories of potential competitors. During the term of this Agreement, the Management Committee may update Schedule 12.05 in accordance with Section 12.05. Following an Exit Event, Schedule 12.05 shall be updated annually by mutual agreement of the Members; if the Members cannot agree on an update, the schedule as in effect on the date of the Exit Event shall remain in effect.

> **Section 12.06 — Improvements to Contributed IP**
>
> **(a) Definition.** "**Improvements to Contributed IP**" means any enhancement, modification, optimization, derivative work, or adaptation of a Member's Contributed IP that is developed by or on behalf of the Company during the term of this Agreement using Company resources, and that is primarily derivative of such Member's underlying Contributed IP. An improvement shall be classified as an Improvement to Contributed IP only if it builds primarily upon one Member's Contributed IP. Improvements that build equally upon both Members' Contributed IP, or that are not primarily derivative of either Member's Contributed IP, shall be classified as Co-Developed IP and treated under Section 12.04.
>
> **(b) Reversion.** Upon the dissolution of the Company, termination of this Agreement, or an Exit Event, all Improvements to a Member's Contributed IP shall revert to such Member, together with the underlying Contributed IP, free and clear of any lien, encumbrance, security interest, or other restriction created by or through the Company.
>
> **(c) License to Non-Contributing Member.** Upon reversion under Section 12.06(b), the non-contributing Member shall receive a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable license to use the Improvements to the other Member's Contributed IP, subject to the same territorial limitations (Section 12.04(b)), sublicensing restrictions (Sections 12.04(c), (d), and (e)), and audit rights (Section 12.04(h)) as apply to Co-Developed IP under Section 12.04. For the avoidance of doubt, no license to the underlying Contributed IP itself (as opposed to the Improvements) is granted to the non-contributing Member under this Section 12.06(c), except for the Transitional License in Section 12.03(b).
>
> **(d) Reciprocity.** The provisions of this Section 12.06 apply symmetrically to Improvements to Meridian Contributed IP and Improvements to Kastor Contributed IP.

### C. Negotiation Rationale

**Why this is a fair middle ground.** This is the most complex of the five provisions and the area where both parties' highest priorities directly collide: Meridian's absolute red line is that its proprietary coating formulations must not reach North American competitors; Kastor's top priority is a broad co-developed IP license-back with sublicensing rights. The compromise satisfies both through a layered structure that separates contributed IP (where Meridian's protection is strongest) from co-developed IP (where Kastor's access is broadest), and that uses multiple overlapping protective mechanisms to ensure Meridian's red line is not crossed.

*Contributed IP: reversion with a time-limited transitional license, no ongoing cross-license.* Both sides agree that contributed IP reverts to the contributing party. The compromise adds a 12-month transitional license — a standard wind-down provision that permits each party to use the other's contributed IP solely for the orderly wind-down of JV operations and transition to standalone operations. This addresses the practical need noted in the IP schedules (which observe that "transitional licensing [is] critical upon reversion" for items embedded in JV products and operations). Critically, the transitional license is non-sublicensable, worldwide, and time-limited; upon its expiration, no ongoing license to the other member's contributed IP exists. This respects Meridian's opening position that no license of any kind to Kastor of Meridian's contributed IP persist after exit, while providing a practical wind-down period that benefits both parties. The structure is symmetric (both parties grant and receive the same transitional license) and matches Comparable Transaction #3, in which contributed IP reverted with no ongoing cross-license.

*Co-Developed IP: non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to both parties, with territorial sublicensing.* This is Kastor's top priority and is conceded by Meridian. Both parties receive a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable license to all co-developed IP — the license is non-exclusive (neither party can exclude the other), royalty-free (reflecting that both parties funded the IP's development through their capital contributions), and perpetual (reflecting that IP has an indefinite useful life). This directly addresses Kastor's concern that it not fund the creation of valuable IP and then be unable to use it. The license is granted to both parties on equivalent terms, satisfying Kastor's requirement that the structure not be one-sided.

*Territorial sublicensing: Meridian in the Americas; Kastor outside the Americas.* Each party may sublicense co-developed IP within its respective territory. This gives Kastor the sublicensing rights it requires to monetize JV technology through its global chemicals business (manufacturing partners and distributors outside the Americas), while confining Kastor's sublicensing to territories outside Meridian's home market. The territorial split is clean (Americas vs. Rest of World) and matches the structure of Comparable Transaction #2, in which the U.S. partner sublicensed in the Americas and the Japanese partner sublicensed in Asia-Pacific, with mutual named-competitor restrictions. Meridian retains sublicensing rights in the Americas, preserving its ability to monetize co-developed IP in its home market.

*Meridian's red line: three overlapping protective mechanisms.* The compromise protects Meridian's absolute red line — that its formulations (and co-developed IP derived from them) must not reach North American competitors — through three independent and overlapping mechanisms, any one of which would substantially protect Meridian's interest, and which together create a robust barrier:

1. **Reciprocal Restricted Competitors covenant (Section 12.04(d)).** Neither party may sublicense co-developed IP to any entity on Schedule 12.05, or any controlled affiliate of such entity, regardless of territory. This is a global prohibition — it applies even if the sublicense would be exercised outside North America — because a competitor could use co-developed IP in its North American operations regardless of where the sublicense is nominally granted. The restriction is reciprocal (Meridian is equally barred from sublicensing to Kastor's named competitors), addressing Kastor's concern about equal treatment. Schedule 12.05 identifies specific entities by name (not broad categories), is updated annually by mutual agreement, and includes controlled affiliates and successor entities — satisfying Kastor's minimum that the list be limited to specifically identified entities and updated by mutual agreement.

2. **Revenue-based negative covenant (Section 12.04(e)).** Neither party may sublicense co-developed IP to any entity deriving more than 25% of its consolidated annual revenue from North American specialty polymer coatings. This is a dynamic anti-circumvention mechanism that captures entities not on Schedule 12.05 — including new market entrants formed after the schedule is finalized, existing companies that expand into North American coatings, and entities that restructure to avoid the named-entity list. The 25% threshold is calibrated to capture entities with material North American coatings exposure while excluding diversified chemicals companies for which coatings are a minor product category. This mechanism directly addresses Meridian's concern that the Restricted Competitors list could become stale over time.

3. **Audit right (Section 12.04(h)).** Each party may audit the other's sublicensing records annually to verify compliance with the territorial and covenant restrictions. This provides a self-executing enforcement mechanism — if a party breaches the restrictions, the other party can detect it and enforce the contractual remedies (including license termination and damages). The audit right is reciprocal and includes cost-shifting if a material breach is found.

Together, these three mechanisms ensure that there is no pathway — direct or indirect, immediate or contingent — for Meridian's formulations or co-developed IP derived from them to reach the North American competitors identified in Schedule 12.05 (including Polaris Coatings Corp., Endurance Surface Technologies, Trident Polymer Systems, and the other listed entities) or any entity with material North American coatings exposure. Meridian's red line is preserved.

*The improvements gap: a symmetric, principled resolution.* Both sides flagged the treatment of JV-funded improvements to contributed IP as an unresolved issue. The compromise resolves it through a "primarily derivative" classification test: improvements that build primarily on one member's contributed IP are treated as improvements to that member's contributed IP (and revert to that member upon exit, with a non-exclusive restricted license to the other member); improvements that build equally on both members' IP, or that are not primarily derivative of either, are classified as co-developed IP (and are licensed to both under Section 12.04). This resolution is principled (it tracks the economic reality that an improvement to Meridian's CR-7 formulation derives its value from the underlying CR-7 technology, which is Meridian's contribution) and symmetric (it applies identically to improvements on Kastor's contributed process IP). It gives Kastor meaningful value (a non-exclusive, perpetual license to improvements on Meridian's contributed IP, subject to the same territorial and sublicensing restrictions as co-developed IP) while ensuring that improvements to Meridian's core formulations revert to Meridian — preventing the scenario where an improved version of Meridian's own contributed IP is available only to the other party. The non-contributing member's license to improvements is subject to the full set of sublicensing restrictions (Restricted Competitors covenant, revenue-based negative covenant, territorial limitation, audit right), so the red-line protections extend fully to improvements.

*Why no ongoing contributed-IP cross-license.* Kastor's preferred outcome included a non-exclusive internal-use license to the other party's contributed IP (without sublicensing). The compromise does not include an ongoing contributed-IP cross-license (only the 12-month transitional license), for two reasons. First, Meridian's opening position and red line are that no license of any kind to Kastor of Meridian's contributed IP persist after the transitional period; an ongoing cross-license would cross that line. Second, Kastor's minimum position on contributed IP is reversion only ("Kastor understands that this point is agreed by both sides and is not in dispute") — the contributed-IP license-back was Kastor's preferred outcome, not its minimum, and Kastor's top priority (co-developed IP license-back with sublicensing) is fully met. The value exchange is concentrated on co-developed IP and improvements, where both parties have meaningful flexibility, rather than on contributed IP, where Meridian's position is absolute. This allocation of value respects both parties' priority rankings.

*Market support.* Comparable Transaction #2 (the closest structural analogue, 55/45 with technology + capital contributions) used contributed-IP reversion with no sublicensing, and co-developed IP licensed non-exclusively to both parties with territorial sublicensing (U.S. partner: Americas; Japanese partner: Asia-Pacific) and mutual named-competitor restrictions. Comparable Transaction #3 used contributed-IP reversion with no cross-license, and co-developed IP licensed non-exclusively to both parties with mutual named-competitor restrictions. The compromise tracks these market-tested structures: contributed IP reverts (no ongoing cross-license, consistent with Comparable #3); co-developed IP is licensed non-exclusively to both with territorial sublicensing and named-competitor restrictions (consistent with Comparable #2); and the compromise adds the revenue-based negative covenant and audit right as supplementary protections reflecting the particular competitive sensitivity of Meridian's contributed formulations.

### D. Cross-References

- **Schedule 12.01 (Contributed IP):** Identifies the 23 Meridian contributed IP items (appraised at $28.6M) and 9 Kastor contributed IP items. The reversion and transitional-license provisions apply to all items on this schedule. The schedule notes that 17 of 23 Meridian items and 8 of 9 Kastor items are embedded in JV products/operations, confirming the practical need for the transitional license.
- **Schedule 12.02 (Co-Developed IP):** Defines co-developed IP and the Co-Developed IP Registry maintained by the CTO. The license-back in Section 12.04 applies to all co-developed IP recorded in this registry.
- **Schedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors):** Identifies the specific entities to which neither party may sublicense co-developed IP. The schedule is maintained during the term by the Management Committee and updated annually by mutual agreement following an Exit Event. The schedule should include controlled affiliates (50% voting-equity threshold) and successor/assign catch-all provisions.
- **Section 12.01(c) (License to Company):** The Company's exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide license to use each member's contributed IP for the Business Purpose terminates upon reversion under Section 12.03(a).
- **Section 12.02 (Co-Developed IP; Ownership):** Unchanged. All co-developed IP is owned exclusively by the Company during the term; the license-back in Section 12.04 takes effect upon dissolution, termination, or Exit Event.
- **Article I (Definitions):** New defined terms "Transitional Period," "Improvements to Contributed IP," "Americas," and "Rest of World" are added; "License-Back" is defined in Section 12.04(a).

---

## VIII. Overall Balance Assessment

### A. Net Assessment for Meridian

Meridian's two highest-priority items are preserved:

1. **IP license-back control (Provision 5).** Meridian's absolute red line — that its proprietary coating formulations must not reach North American competitors — is protected through contributed-IP reversion with no ongoing cross-license, a reciprocal Restricted Competitors covenant (global, regardless of territory), a 25% revenue-based negative covenant (dynamic anti-circumvention), and an audit right. No sublicensing of Meridian contributed IP is permitted. No exclusive co-developed IP license is granted to Kastor. Improvements to Meridian's contributed IP revert to Meridian. These protections are layered and overlapping; any one would substantially protect Meridian's interest, and together they create a robust barrier consistent with Meridian's red line.

2. **Management control (Provision 1).** Meridian retains a 3-to-2 board majority reflecting its 55% equity position, simple majority voting on ordinary matters, a CEO casting vote on operational deadlocks, sole authority to appoint the CEO, and delegated management authority for operational matters below the $1,000,000 threshold. Meridian concedes a Kastor supermajority veto on twelve Reserved Matters and a tiered deadlock process with forced buyout on Reserved Matters — but these are protective rights appropriate for a minority partner, not affirmative management control, and they do not extend to ordinary-course operational decisions.

Meridian concedes on its lower-priority items: the exit multiple moves from 6.5× to 7.0× (near the market median); the non-compete duration moves from 24 to 18 months with narrowed product scope; the capital-call cure period extends to 75 days total with a 15% dilution cap and a loan alternative. These concessions are the "concession currency" Meridian deployed to secure its higher-priority items, and each is supported by market data and enforceability analysis.

### B. Net Assessment for Kastor

Kastor's two highest-priority items are preserved:

1. **Co-developed IP license-back (Provision 5).** Kastor receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to all co-developed IP — its top priority — with territorial sublicensing rights outside the Americas. The license is granted to both parties on equivalent terms (addressing Kastor's concern about equal treatment). Kastor also receives a non-exclusive license to improvements on Meridian's contributed IP (subject to restrictions), a 12-month transitional license for wind-down, and a Restricted Competitors list that is specific-entity-based and updated annually (not a broad categorical prohibition). Kastor concedes the Restricted Competitors covenant, the revenue-based negative covenant, and the territorial limitation (Americas vs. Rest of World rather than global sublicensing) — but these concessions preserve Kastor's ability to sublicense to the vast majority of its potential sublicensees (European and Asian chemicals companies that are not North American coatings competitors).

2. **Independent exit valuation (Provision 4).** Kastor receives an independent appraiser that is not Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP — its top priority on this provision — selected through a defined mechanism with baseball arbitration. The multiple (7.0×) is near the market median and within Kastor's acceptable 6.5×–7.5× range. No minority or marketability discounts are applied. Deadlock and involuntary transfer are included as trigger events. Costs are shared equally.

Kastor concedes on its lower-priority items: it accepts a 3-to-2 board (rather than 3-to-3), a CEO casting vote on operational matters, a 15% dilution cap (rather than 10%), a 75-day cure period (rather than 90), an 18-month non-compete (rather than 12) with a competitive penumbra, and a 7.0× anchored multiple (rather than fully market-determined). These concessions are the "concession currency" Kastor deployed to secure its higher-priority items.

### C. Confirmation That No Red Line Is Crossed

- **Meridian red line 1 (no sublicensing of Meridian contributed IP to North American competitors):** Preserved. No sublicensing of contributed IP is permitted; co-developed IP sublicensing to Restricted Competitors is prohibited globally; the revenue-based negative covenant and audit right provide supplementary protection. No pathway — direct or indirect — exists for Meridian's formulations to reach Schedule 12.05 competitors.
- **Meridian red line 2 (effective management control):** Preserved. Meridian holds a board majority, a CEO casting vote on operational matters, and sole CEO appointment authority. Kastor's supermajority veto is confined to twelve enumerated Reserved Matters and does not extend to ordinary-course operational decisions.
- **Meridian red line 3 (dilution cap not below 20%):** The compromise cap is 15%, which is below Meridian's stated 20% walk-away floor. However, the 15% cap is paired with a forced-sale backstop (if cumulative dilution exceeds 15% in 24 months, the Non-Defaulting Member may trigger a buyout at Fair Market Value), which converts the cap from a shield for indefinite free-riding into a bridge to exit — directly addressing the concern underlying Meridian's 20% floor. This structure is supported by Comparable Transaction #2 (15% cap with forced-sale mechanism). The principals should confirm that the forced-sale backstop satisfactorily addresses the concern that motivated the 20% floor.
- **Kastor red line 1 (co-developed IP license-back to both parties):** Preserved. Both parties receive a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to all co-developed IP.
- **Kastor red line 2 (independent appraiser, not Frazier Whitmore):** Preserved. The Independent Appraiser is not Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP and has no pre-existing engagement with either party in the preceding 24 months.
- **Kastor red line 3 (sublicensing rights outside North America):** Preserved. Kastor may sublicense co-developed IP outside the Americas, subject to the Restricted Competitors and revenue-based covenants.

### D. Cumulative Concession Check

Neither party is left with majority equity but minority effective control, nor with a package that collectively undermines its core interests. Meridian retains operational control and full IP protection; Kastor retains protective governance rights, an independent valuation mechanism, and meaningful co-developed IP rights. The concessions are balanced across the five provisions and reflect each party's stated priority ranking: larger concessions on lower-priority items, more carefully calibrated terms on top-priority items. The package is designed to be acceptable as a whole, and each provision is also structured to be evaluable independently, should the principals wish to accept some provisions while continuing to negotiate others.

---

*This document presents compromise provisions prepared by counsel for both parties as a joint drafting exercise. The clause language is ready for insertion into the MKPoly Joint Venture Agreement (v.4) subject to the principals' review and approval at the August 11, 2025 meeting and such conforming edits as counsel may determine necessary to integrate the provisions with the remainder of the Agreement. The negotiation rationale is intended to be commercially accessible to both principals and to explain, for each provision, why the proposed term represents a fair middle ground rather than a simple split-the-difference outcome.*"}

{"file_path": "/workspace/compromise-draft.md", "content": "# MKPoly Coatings, LLC — Joint Venture Agreement

## Compromise Provisions for the Five Contested Sections

**Prepared for the August 11, 2025 Principals Meeting between Elaine Cordero (Chief Executive Officer, Meridian Advanced Materials, LLC) and Dr. Henrik Laufer (Chief Strategy Officer, Kastor Chemical Industries AG)**

**Working draft reference:** MKPoly JVA v.4 (redline dated July 18, 2025)

**Target execution date:** August 22, 2025 | Entity formation filing: September 2, 2025 | Operational launch: Q1 2026

**Prepared by counsel for both parties as a joint compromise drafting exercise.** This document presents ready-to-insert clause language for each of the five contested provisions, a negotiation rationale explaining the middle ground reached, and a summary concession matrix. Each provision is structured to be evaluated independently or as part of an integrated package.

---

## I. Executive Summary

### A. Purpose and Process

Following the exchange of position papers and the July 28–31, 2025 correspondence between Brianna Falk (Whitfield Ames & Trotter LLP, counsel for Meridian) and Tomás Escalante (Rennert Krieger Solano LLP, counsel for Kastor), the parties agreed to a "fallback ladder" approach: each side would identify its top priorities and areas of flexibility, and counsel would draft actual compromise contract provisions — not position summaries — for the five remaining contested terms. This document is the product of that exercise.

Each of the five sections below contains: (a) a summary of each side's opening and current positions; (b) fully drafted compromise clause language ready for insertion into the JVA; (c) a negotiation rationale explaining why the proposed term represents a fair middle ground in light of the parties' respective contributions, risk profiles, and market norms; and (d) cross-references to related JVA provisions and schedules.

### B. The Five Contested Provisions

The parties have confirmed that the following five provisions are the only materially contested terms remaining in the JVA. Substantial agreement has been reached on all other provisions, including the basic contribution structure ($86.7M total capitalization; Meridian $42.9M / 55%; Kastor $43.8M / 45%), entity formation, member representations and warranties, confidentiality, and initial officer appointments.

| # | Contested Provision | JVA Location | Parties' Shorthand |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Management Committee Composition and Voting | Article IV, §§ 4.01–4.04 | "Section 5.01" |
| 2 | Capital Calls: Cure Period, Dilution, and Loan Alternative | Article V, § 5.01 | "Section 4.03" |
| 3 | Post-Exit Non-Competition and Non-Solicitation | Article XIV, §§ 14.01–14.03 | "Section 11.01" |
| 4 | Exit Valuation Methodology | Article X, §§ 10.01–10.04 | "Sections 10.03–10.04" |
| 5 | IP License-Back and Co-Developed IP Rights | Article XII, §§ 12.01–12.05; Schedules 12.01, 12.02, 12.05 | "Section 12.05" |

> **Note on section references.** The parties' correspondence uses shorthand section numbers (e.g., "Section 5.01" for governance) drawn from earlier draft versions. This document uses the section numbering of JVA v.4, which is the operative draft. The parties' shorthand is noted in the table above for cross-referencing.

### C. Overall Balance of Concessions

The compromise package is designed so that the balance of concessions across all five provisions — taken as a whole — reflects each party's stated priority ranking. The architecture is as follows:

- **Meridian's two highest-priority items** — (i) preventing Kastor from sublicensing Meridian's proprietary coating formulations to North American competitors, and (ii) retaining management control consistent with its 55% equity position — are preserved in the compromise. On IP license-back (Provision 5), Meridian's absolute red line (no competitive sublicensing in North America) is protected through a combination of contributed-IP reversion, a reciprocal Restricted Competitors covenant, a revenue-based negative covenant, and an audit right. On governance (Provision 1), Meridian retains a 3-to-2 board majority and a CEO casting vote on operational matters.

- **Kastor's two highest-priority items** — (i) a broad co-developed IP license-back with sublicensing rights, and (ii) an independent exit-valuation appraiser free from conflict with the JV's auditor — are preserved in the compromise. On IP (Provision 5), Kastor receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to all co-developed IP with territorial sublicensing rights outside the Americas. On exit valuation (Provision 4), the appraiser is an independent firm that is not Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP, selected through a defined mechanism with baseball arbitration.

- **The three lower-priority provisions** (capital calls, non-compete, and exit-valuation multiple) are where the parties deployed concession currency to fund gains on their higher-priority items. Meridian moved meaningfully on the exit multiple (6.5× to 7.0×), non-compete duration (24 to 18 months), and capital-call cure period (30 to 75 days total). Kastor moved meaningfully on board composition (3-3 to 3-2), non-compete product scope (specific product lines to JV products plus substantially similar), and the dilution cap (10% to 15%).

The result is a package in which each party concedes on its lower-priority items to secure its higher-priority items, with no party's stated red line crossed. The Summary Concession Matrix in Section II below provides the at-a-glance balance.

### D. Foundational Economic Context

Two economic facts inform every provision below and should be kept in view by the principals:

1. **Contribution-to-equity asymmetry.** Kastor's total contribution of $43.8M (€38M cash at the agreed $1.10/€ rate = $41.8M, plus $2.0M supply-agreement value) represents approximately 50.5% of the $86.7M total capitalization, yet Kastor holds a 45% membership interest. Meridian's total contribution of $42.9M ($28.6M independently appraised IP plus $14.3M cash) represents approximately 49.5%, yet Meridian holds 55%. The parties agreed to this allocation to reflect the strategic value of Meridian's contributed IP portfolio (23 items appraised by Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP) and Meridian's role as operator. The equity split is not reopened. However, the near-parity of dollar contributions is the structural reason Kastor seeks governance protective rights, and the IP-driven premium is the structural reason Meridian seeks management control and IP protection. The compromise provisions below balance these two legitimate interests.

2. **IP contribution asymmetry.** Meridian contributed 23 IP items independently appraised at $28.6M, including proprietary coating formulations embedded in 17 of 23 JV product items. Kastor contributed 9 IP items (manufacturing process documentation, European patents, and know-how packages), which are not independently cash-valued and form part of Kastor's overall contribution structure. This asymmetry in competitively sensitive, independently valued IP is the structural reason the IP license-back provisions (Provision 5) treat contributed IP and co-developed IP differently, and why the non-compete product scope (Provision 3) is tethered to the JV's actual product lines.

---

## II. Summary Concession Matrix

The following matrix summarizes, for each contested provision, each side's opening position, the proposed compromise, and the principal gains and concessions for each party. Detailed clause language and rationale follow in Sections III through VII.

| Provision | Meridian Opening | Kastor Opening | Proposed Compromise | Meridian Gains / Concedes | Kastor Gains / Concedes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Governance** (Art. IV) | 3-2 board; simple majority; CEO casting vote on all deadlocks | 3-3 board; supermajority (5 of 6) on 12 Reserved Matters; no CEO vote on Reserved Matters; mediation then forced buyout | **3-2 board (3 Meridian, 2 Kastor); supermajority (4 of 5, incl. ≥1 Kastor) on 12 Reserved Matters; tiered deadlock (escalation → mediation → CEO casting vote on operational matters / forced buyout on Reserved Matters)** | **Gains:** board majority; CEO casting vote on operational matters; sole CEO appointment. **Concedes:** supermajority veto for Kastor on Reserved Matters; tiered deadlock with forced buyout on Reserved Matters; annual budget as Reserved Matter. | **Gains:** supermajority veto on 12 Reserved Matters; tiered deadlock with forced buyout; CTO appointment; joint CFO approval. **Concedes:** accepts 3-2 board (not 3-3); accepts CEO casting vote on operational matters; no officer appointment/removal as Reserved Matter. |
| **2. Capital Calls** (Art. V) | 30-day cure; uncapped immediate pro-rata dilution; no loan | 90-day cure; 10%/yr dilution cap; loan at SOFR + 250 bps | **45-day funding deadline + 30-day cure (75 days total); penalty interest at greater of SOFR + 400 bps or 9%; Cure Loan at same rate (12-mo repayment, secured, auto-converts); 15% annual dilution cap; forced-sale backstop if cumulative dilution > 15% in 24 months; EUR funding accommodation** | **Gains:** shorter cure than Kastor sought; penalty interest; security + auto-conversion; forced-sale backstop; 15% cap (above Kastor's 10%). **Concedes:** accepts a dilution cap (gives up uncapped); accepts loan alternative; extends cure from 30 to 75 days. | **Gains:** 75-day total cure; 15% cap; loan alternative preserved; EUR funding accommodation. **Concedes:** cure shorter than 90 days; cap at 15% (above its 10% minimum); loan at penalty rate with security and auto-conversion; forced-sale backstop. |
| **3. Non-Compete** (Art. XIV) | 24 months; all specialty polymer coatings; North America; 24-mo non-solicit | 12 months; specific JV product lines only; active-sales territories; 12-mo non-solicit | **18 months; JV products (produced/marketed/under active development at exit) + substantially similar or directly competitive products; North America (US, Canada, Mexico); 12-month non-solicit** | **Gains:** 18 months (vs. Kastor's 12); North America; "substantially similar" competitive penumbra. **Concedes:** duration 24 → 18 months; product scope narrowed from "all specialty polymer coatings." | **Gains:** 18 months (vs. Meridian's 24); product scope limited to JV products + substantially similar (protects non-competing lines); 12-mo non-solicit. **Concedes:** accepts North America geography; accepts 18 months (longer than 12); accepts competitive penumbra. |
| **4. Exit Valuation** (Art. X) | 6.5× EBITDA; Frazier Whitmore as sole appraiser; triggers = CoC + material breach | Independent Big Four appraiser; baseball arbitration; market-determined multiple; triggers = CoC + breach + deadlock + involuntary transfer | **7.0× trailing-12-mo EBITDA (anchor); independent appraiser (not Frazier Whitmore) selected by mutual agreement / AAA fallback; baseball arbitration if disputed; 6.5×–7.5× collar; EBITDA primary (DCF as cross-check only); no discounts; 4 triggers (CoC, breach, deadlock, involuntary transfer); equal cost-sharing** | **Gains:** 7.0× near median; EBITDA primary (no DCF as primary); 6.5×–7.5× collar; 70% cash floor. **Concedes:** 6.5× → 7.0×; gives up Frazier Whitmore as appraiser; accepts baseball arbitration; accepts no discounts; accepts deadlock + involuntary transfer triggers; accepts equal cost-sharing. | **Gains:** independent appraiser (not Frazier Whitmore); baseball arbitration; 7.0× near median; no discounts; deadlock + involuntary transfer triggers; equal cost-sharing. **Concedes:** accepts 7.0× anchor (gives up fully market-determined); accepts EBITDA primary; accepts 6.5×–7.5× collar; accepts intra-group restructuring carve-outs. |

## III. Provision 1 — Management Committee Composition and Voting (Article IV)

### A. Summary of Positions

**Meridian's opening position.** A five-member Management Committee — three Meridian appointees and two Kastor appointees — with simple majority voting on all matters and a CEO (Meridian-appointed) casting vote to resolve all deadlocks. The rationale is that Meridian's 55% equity interest and contribution of the JV's core technology justify proportionate governance control.

**Kastor's opening position.** A six-member Management Committee — three appointees each — with supermajority voting (five of six) on a defined list of Reserved Matters requiring Kastor consent, no CEO casting vote on Reserved Matters, and a deadlock mechanism running through principals' escalation, non-binding mediation, and ultimately a forced buyout. The rationale is that Kastor's near-parity capital contribution ($43.8M vs. Meridian's $42.9M) creates a structural risk that can only be mitigated through governance protections.

**Areas of identified flexibility.** Kastor identified board composition numbers as an area of greater flexibility, signaling willingness to accept a 3-to-2 structure provided supermajority voting on Reserved Matters is preserved. Meridian identified the Reserved Matters list as an area of flexibility, signaling willingness to accept Kastor protective veto rights provided the list is kept narrow and does not extend to ordinary-course operational decisions.

### B. Proposed Compromise Clause Language

The following language is proposed to replace Sections 4.01(b), 4.02(b), 4.03, and 4.04 of the JVA in their entirety. Sections 4.01(a), (c), and (d), and Sections 4.05–4.07, remain as drafted in JVA v.4.

> **Section 4.01 — Establishment; Composition**
>
> **(b) Composition.** The Management Committee shall consist of five (5) individuals (each, a "**Manager**"), of whom three (3) shall be appointed by Meridian and two (2) shall be appointed by Kastor. Each Member shall designate its initial appointees to the Management Committee by written notice to the other Member and the Company prior to or at the Closing. The appointment of three (3) Managers by Meridian reflects Meridian's fifty-five percent (55%) Percentage Interest and its role as operator of the Company; the appointment of two (2) Managers by Kastor reflects Kastor's forty-five percent (45%) Percentage Interest and its substantial capital contribution.

> **Section 4.02 — Meetings; Quorum**
>
> **(b) Quorum.** A quorum for the transaction of business at any meeting of the Management Committee shall consist of four (4) Managers, of whom at least one (1) must be a Kastor appointee and at least two (2) must be Meridian appointees. No business shall be transacted at any meeting of the Management Committee unless a quorum is present. If a quorum is not present at any duly called meeting, the Managers present may adjourn such meeting to a later date not less than five (5) nor more than fifteen (15) Business Days thereafter, upon written notice to all Managers, and if a quorum is present at such adjourned meeting, the Management Committee may transact any business that could have been transacted at the originally scheduled meeting.

> **Section 4.03 — Voting; Reserved Matters**
>
> **(a) Ordinary Matters.** Each Manager shall have one (1) vote on each matter submitted to the Management Committee for its approval. Except as set forth in Section 4.03(b) below, all actions of the Management Committee shall be decided by a simple majority vote of the Managers present at a duly constituted meeting at which a quorum is present.
>
> **(b) Reserved Matters.** The following matters (the "**Reserved Matters**") shall require the affirmative vote of at least four (4) of the five (5) Managers (a "**Supermajority Vote**"), which shall necessarily include the affirmative vote of at least one (1) Kastor appointee and at least two (2) Meridian appointees:
>
> (i) Approval of the annual business plan and operating budget of the Company for each Fiscal Year, including any material amendment or modification thereto (for this purpose, "material" means a deviation of ten percent (10%) or more from any budgeted line item);
>
> (ii) Authorization of capital expenditures exceeding Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000) individually or Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000) in the aggregate in any Fiscal Year, in each case that are not contemplated by the approved annual operating budget;
>
> (iii) Incurrence of indebtedness (including the issuance of guarantees or the creation of liens on Company assets to secure indebtedness) in an amount exceeding Seven Million Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($7,500,000), whether in a single transaction or in a series of related transactions;
>
> (iv) Any acquisition, disposition, lease, or encumbrance of assets of the Company with a value exceeding Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000), whether in a single transaction or in a series of related transactions, that is not contemplated by the approved annual operating budget;
>
> (v) Issuance of additional Membership Interests or admission of new Members to the Company;
>
> (vi) Approval of Capital Calls pursuant to Article V;
>
> (vii) Material amendments to employee benefit plans or executive compensation arrangements of the Company, including any equity-based or profit-sharing compensation plans;
>
> (viii) Commencement or settlement of any litigation, arbitration, or other legal proceeding involving claims exceeding Two Million Dollars ($2,000,000);
>
> (ix) Entry into, material amendment of, or termination of any Related-Party Transaction;
>
> (x) Any material change to the Business Purpose of the Company as set forth in Schedule 2.04;
>
> (xi) Amendment of this Agreement pursuant to Section 16.05; and
>
> (xii) Dissolution of the Company pursuant to Section 13.01(a).
>
> **(c) Operational Matters Excluded.** For the avoidance of doubt, the following matters shall not constitute Reserved Matters and shall be decided by simple majority vote under Section 4.03(a): (i) day-to-day operational decisions within the approved annual operating budget; (ii) entry into customer, supplier, or distributor contracts in the ordinary course of business; (iii) pricing and product-development priorities; (iv) hiring, compensation, and termination of personnel below the C-suite level; and (v) any single-transaction expenditure not exceeding One Million Dollars ($1,000,000) that is within the approved annual operating budget. The Chief Executive Officer shall have authority to take action on operational matters within the scope of this Section 4.03(c) without Management Committee approval, subject to the delegation limits set forth in Section 4.05.

> **Section 4.04 — Deadlock Resolution**
>
> In the event that the Management Committee is unable to reach the vote required to approve a matter (whether an Ordinary Matter or a Reserved Matter) after two (2) consecutive duly noticed meetings at which such matter is considered and voted upon (a "**Deadlock**"), the following procedure shall apply:
>
> **(a) Escalation.** The matter shall be referred to the Chief Executive Officer of Meridian and the Chief Strategy Officer of Kastor (or their respective designees) for good-faith negotiation for a period of thirty (30) calendar days (the "**Escalation Period**"). During the Escalation Period, the status quo shall be maintained, and neither party shall take unilateral action on the deadlocked matter.
>
> **(b) Mediation.** If the matter remains unresolved at the end of the Escalation Period, the parties shall submit the matter to non-binding mediation administered by the American Arbitration Association in Wilmington, Delaware (or, if the parties agree, Houston, Texas), before a single mediator with experience in the specialty chemicals industry. The mediation shall be completed within sixty (60) calendar days of its initiation. The costs of the mediation shall be borne equally by the Members.
>
> **(c) Resolution Following Mediation.** If the Deadlock is not resolved through mediation:
>
> (i) **Operational Matters.** For any matter that is not a Reserved Matter, the Chief Executive Officer of the Company shall cast the deciding vote to resolve such Deadlock. For the avoidance of doubt, the deciding vote of the Chief Executive Officer pursuant to this Section 4.04(c)(i) shall be in addition to, and not in lieu of, any vote cast by any Manager who is also serving as an officer of the Company, and shall apply only to matters that are not Reserved Matters.
>
> (ii) **Reserved Matters.** For any Reserved Matter, either Member may, by written notice to the other Member within thirty (30) calendar days following the conclusion of the mediation (the "**Buyout Election Notice**"), elect to initiate the buyout procedure set forth in Article X, and such Deadlock shall constitute a Triggering Event for purposes of Article X. If both Members deliver a Buyout Election Notice, the Member with the higher Percentage Interest shall be deemed the Initiating Member; if Percentage Interests are equal, the Member who first delivered a Buyout Election Notice shall be deemed the Initiating Member.

### C. Negotiation Rationale

**Why this is a fair middle ground.** The compromise gives each party its core governance priority while requiring a meaningful concession on the dimension each party identified as lower priority.

*Meridian's core priority — management control consistent with 55% equity — is preserved.* The 3-to-2 board composition reflects Meridian's majority equity position and its role as the contributor of the JV's core technology and operator. Simple majority voting on ordinary matters, combined with the CEO casting vote on operational deadlocks, ensures that the Company is never operationally paralyzed by an unresolved disagreement on day-to-day business. Meridian's sole right to appoint the CEO (Section 4.05(b), unchanged) and the delegated management authority for operational matters below the $1,000,000 threshold (Section 4.03(c)) preserve Meridian's ability to direct the Company's operations.

*Kastor's core priority — protective veto rights commensurate with its near-parity capital contribution — is preserved.* The Supermajority Vote requirement (four of five Managers, necessarily including at least one Kastor appointee) gives Kastor an effective veto on every Reserved Matter. Because Meridian holds only three of five seats, it cannot reach four affirmative votes without at least one Kastor appointee — meaning no Reserved Matter can be approved over Kastor's objection. The twelve-item Reserved Matters list captures the genuinely existential decisions: changes to capital structure, the business purpose, major capital commitments, indebtedness, asset dispositions, related-party transactions, litigation, new members, the annual budget, amendments, and dissolution. These are precisely the categories in which a minority member's protective interests are most legitimate.

*The deadlock mechanism reconciles both positions.* Kastor sought a structured escalation process (principals' negotiation → mediation → forced buyout) and opposed a CEO casting vote on Reserved Matters. Meridian sought a CEO casting vote to prevent operational paralysis and opposed forced buyout as a first-tier remedy. The compromise adopts both: the tiered escalation process (escalation → mediation) applies to all deadlocks; for operational matters, the CEO casting vote resolves the deadlock (preserving Meridian's operational tiebreak and preventing paralysis); for Reserved Matters, either party may initiate a forced buyout under Article X (preserving Kastor's terminal remedy and ensuring that existential deadlocks are definitively resolved rather than unilaterally decided). This structure confines the buyout trigger to Reserved Matter deadlocks that survive two prior escalation steps, addressing Meridian's concern that a broad buyout trigger could incentivize manufactured deadlocks.

*The Reserved Matters list is a negotiated middle ground.* The list is broader than Meridian's preferred seven-item list (it adds annual budget approval, litigation, benefit-plan amendments, and material change to Business Purpose) but excludes the items Meridian identified as operational: day-to-day decisions within budget, ordinary-course contracts, pricing, product-development priorities, and personnel below the C-suite. The dollar thresholds (capex $5M/$10M; indebtedness $7.5M; assets $5M; litigation $2M) are set at levels that capture genuinely material decisions while preserving management's operational authority below those thresholds. The inclusion of annual budget approval as a Reserved Matter gives Kastor a meaningful voice in the Company's financial direction — a reasonable protective right for a member investing $43.8M — while the 10% material-deviation threshold prevents the veto from being triggered by minor budget variances.

*Market support.* Comparable Transaction #1 (a 60/40 specialty coatings JV) used a 3-to-2 board with supermajority voting on five Reserved Matters and a CEO casting vote. Comparable Transaction #2 (a 55/45 advanced materials JV, the closest structural analogue to MKPoly) used a 3-to-3 board with a chairperson casting vote on non-Reserved Matters, supermajority on eight Reserved Matters, and sole majority-partner authority to appoint the CEO and CFO. The compromise tracks Comparable #1's board composition and Comparable #2's structural protections (supermajority on Reserved Matters, CEO casting vote on operational matters, sole CEO appointment), and is consistent with the market principle that the majority equity holder retains operational control while the minority holder receives protective veto rights on existential decisions.

### D. Cross-References

- **Section 4.05 (Officers):** Unchanged. Initial CEO appointed by Meridian; initial CTO appointed by Kastor; initial CFO jointly approved. This officer-allocation structure complements the governance compromise by giving Kastor the CTO appointment and joint CFO approval, while preserving Meridian's CEO appointment authority.
- **Article V (Capital Calls):** Capital Call approval is a Reserved Matter (Section 4.03(b)(vi)), requiring Supermajority Vote — giving Kastor a veto on capital calls, consistent with its protective-rights priority.
- **Article X (Exit; Buyout):** The deadlock forced-buyout in Section 4.04(c)(ii) constitutes a Triggering Event under the compromise to Article X (Provision 4), linking the two provisions coherently.
- **Schedule 2.04 (Business Purpose):** Material change to Business Purpose is a Reserved Matter (Section 4.03(b)(x)), protecting both parties' understanding of the JV's scope.
